speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 3, August 16, 1995. I am interviewing Wilhelmina De Groot. My name is Stella [? Eliezrie. ?] And we are in Murrieta Hot Springs, California. OK. Mimi, would you tell us the story about Jack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One day, I remember my friend got-- my good friend got very, very sick. And she needed some yeast, and she couldn't get that anywhere. And I knew a place where I could get it in a brewery-- in a brewery where they make the beer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with the children to get her a bottle of this stuff. And while I was standing there, a man came and stood next to me. And I knew him at once because he used to be a porter in nightclubs before the war, where all the Jewish people used to go. So he knew a lot of Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to be-- he used to get 600 francs per person to give them to-- give them over to the Germans, to betray them to the Germans-- where he got all that money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was standing next to me, and he was looking at me. I was looking down. I was laughing and talking, but I knew exactly who he was and what he was doing. So I stayed there. I got my yeast. I talked to him a little while. Then I turned around with my children, went back to the door, thinking any moment I'd feel his hand on my shoulder.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he didn't recognize me because I had my hair long. My children were very, very blonde. So he didn't recognize me. So that's the way I got away from that one. That was a very, very close shave.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Jack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he got murdered after the war. People-- everybody knew him. They killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jews killed him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what his last name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But I know he sent his wife and six children to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: From everybody-- everybody knew about it after the war. He got 600 francs for everybody he sent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. Tell us, please, now, going back to your time after your city was liberated, did you decide-- did you remain in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to-- we remained in Brussels. We wanted to go to England, but I had to get all my quotas, all the papers ready, papers of good conduct throughout the war, that I didn't go with Germans, or we weren't friends with Germans. We had to get all the papers together. Then we got our quota. And then, we went to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to go to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to stay in Brussels anyone, in case something worse happened. England is a big country. Brussels is quickly overbear-- overborne by anything. So we wanted to live in England. That's why we went to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. Was your family still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother, my brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder how they managed, knowing that you were in Belgium during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they-- my mother-- my mother wrote a letter to the pope once--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she say in that letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Asking him for his prayers for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did she write to the pope?

speaker: interviewee
text: What does anybody do when they're miserable, and they don't know what to do? They think anything will help.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know if she received an answer, do you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she receive an answer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she feel--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I know she always told me that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she feel that the pope would be compassionate to you, as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, it might help that the pope, he's such a big name, that a pope might be able to do something. But, of course, nobody was-- nobody could do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the church in, in Brussels take in any Jewish people in hiding? Do you know of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. Probably some of them did. I don't really know because we didn't go to church, or we didn't-- you know. My friends didn't either.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the Catholic school that your children-- I'm going back for a moment-- but in the Catholic school that your children attended, did they ever hear things or come home and tell you about things they might have heard against the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. I don't-- they didn't know my children were Jewish. They were so blonde when they were small. They didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you went to England. That was in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We didn't get a quota until, I think, two years after. It was difficult to get the quota to go because I was English, my husband wasn't, and the children weren't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I suppose it was '45, '46. It must have been near about '47 when we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you-- what did you and your husband do for a living when you arrived in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, see, for six months, we couldn't do anything. I had a friend who helped us there. And my-- we lived with my mother. She had a little house there. And my brothers were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you put the children in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One went to Gregg's College and the other went to Clark's College.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are those?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're, they're, they're very nice schools in England. One was-- they had these little caps on, you know, red caps and the red coat. They have the uniform clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And after you had passed those six months in England, then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: After England?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my husband didn't like living in England, so we went back to Brussels, to Brussels. And we lived in Brussels until we came-- until we went to Canada. And from Canada, we came here 30 years ago. And here we're going to stay until we die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you choose California to come to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I came here once, with an aunt, after the war. I was here for six months with her, and we enjoyed it so much. We loved it. And I always swore, when I get back, the thing I'm going to do is get papers and come to California because Canada was so cold. For a woman, it's murder, Canada. It's so cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you support yourselves when you came to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: How?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: We opened a small factory with the vestments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain that to us? What were you manufacturing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vestments for the Catholic churches.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what you saw in the-- in the pages there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very elaborate, very plain ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what made you get interested in that kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't interested, really. It was a friend of Louie, he used to know as a school friend. And he lived in New York. And he asked Louie if he'd like to be a salesman for him, for that, to do that. And Louie said yes, anything-- anything, I'll do anything. So that's why we came to Canada, and we worked in Canada for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you successful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We earned a living. Louie earned a living. And then, he worked for a Jewish friend we had in Brussels, during the war. And he lived in Canada. And he took Louie on as a salesman. And Louie-- he was very pleased with Louie. He earned a good living with this guy, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't work until I came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I worked with Louie doing the designing. And I didn't want to be alone at home. The children were married, grown up, have children. I didn't want to be alone, so I went-- so we went to work together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is it that you designed?

speaker: interviewee
text: We traveled together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you design in the clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the, the designs for the Catholic clothes and stuck them on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find it odd as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: --cutting them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. If it had been-- if it had been Jewish designs, with Jewish things, I would have done exactly the same thing. I wouldn't have thought that's strange, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were making a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think-- people's-- I think God is one person. If it's Jewish, or Catholic, or Buddha, or anything-- for me, it's one. It's only one person who's supposed to be good. And there's only one person who can do the things that should be done for the whole world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there can only be one. Why all this fighting between Jews and Catholics? Why do they hate each other? Why? Only because one is called Catholic and the other is called a Jew, that's all. That's why they fight. Tell me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Why can't it all be the same? Why can't everybody be the same? Why can't people like dark people, yellow people, black people, why do we make a difference because people have got a different skin, and this black man has a different skin? Why should there be such a hate? Why?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- perhaps it's unfair to ask, but do you see now, after the war-- can you forgive the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you accept them as another?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can forgive the kids. Kids can't help what their parents do. So you don't have to be mad with the young people today who are Germans. And you can't really be mad at all the Germans because they had their orders, and they had to do what Hitler said, too. Otherwise, they would have gone to the camps too, which many of them did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what do you-- I hate-- what do you do now for pleasure at this stage in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we play cards a little bit, and we-- I have my children next door. They keep us so busy. My son keeps us busy, from out of Los Angeles. My grandchildren come up practically every week. And my grand-- great grandchild comes up two or three times a month. And we're kept busy. And my husband cooks every night, and I clean up. And we go shopping every day, food shopping.

speaker: interviewer
text: If there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds like a good life.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would like to share with your children? Having lived through all the things that you've lived through, is there something that you would like to share with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think my granddaughter, who got married on our-- the 10th of September marriage day, when we got married. She got married on that date because she would like to have the same life and live with her husband as long as we. And my youngest son is getting married on the 10th of September, on our date, so that he can follow in our footsteps and also be married as long as we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it that you did right in life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? Who knows? For somebody, maybe I did it all wrong. For another person, maybe I did it all right. Who knows?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think that's it. I think that's good. That's very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now? This is my mother, Clara [? Deermont. ?] She was 18 years old when this picture was taken. She lived in Antwerp. She was known as the most beautiful girl of Antwerp. She wasn't allowed out anywhere without an escort. She was a very, very, very, very good mother, gave me lots of good advice. She was 83 when she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken? How old was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken at Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. About how long ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18. She was years 18 years old. She was 83 when she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was my father, Samuel Jacobs. He was a musician, cellist, the first cellist in London. He used to play in all the big shows in Piccadilly. He was 54 when he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my marriage bouquet when it-- it's 60 years old. It was buried during the whole war, in the backyard, in a trunk in my friend's house. And she gave it back after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our-- this is our marriage license, 60 years old, also buried in a garden in my friend's house till after the war. The name of my two sons, Ronald and Jack, are entered into it also.

speaker: interviewer
text: The handkerchief.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are my two sons, Ronald and Jack. And they were born June-- Jack was born-- Ronnie was born June the 23rd and Jack, the 27th of October. They were with us during the whole war. And that's a sort of handkerchief they gave to me at that age, saved a lot of trouble and saved a lot of trouble to bring it to us, a Mother's Day handkerchief.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest one is Jack, Monique. The other children are my eldest son, Ron. Chantalle, Natalie, Jason, are the children of my youngest son. The other guy is Tony, who is now the husband of my granddaughter, Monique.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are all your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all, isn't it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are they? Your grand--

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all taken here on a cruise, going to Mexico.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a cruise, going to Mexico.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long ago was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This is here, in San Pedro.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not so long ago. That was two years ago, Lou?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years ago, OK. And go ahead, Mimi. Introduce him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to introduce to you to my husband, Louis de Groot. This is the one who had that terrible time in the camp and dragged us all through the war. I don't know how he did it with a wife and two children, but he was very, very courageous. And I love him very dearly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell you something, this is just an interview. I-- after, after the war, I go, and she want to travel with me, that she didn't leave me alone. And we go to Newfoundland. And there was ice, and the car slipped. And we fell in a ravine, 60 feet down over. She  was unconscious, broken neck. And I crawled up 70 feet. And I put on my coat on the road. And people found us six hours later. We brought her on top. We go to the hospital. I walk to the hospital. And the next morn-- the evening, I found out all my ribs were broken. But we're still here, and we're still married.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're still married, 60 years married. My granddaughter got married on the 10th of September, to be like us. Now, my youngest son is getting married on the 10th of September-- on the 10th of September-- to be married on our anniversary, 60 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's a long time, but we keep it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the end, end-- there's no end. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: There's an end to everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no end.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's an end to everything, the good and the bad. Everything passes, right? Yes. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think--
